headline: The Wombats Keep Finding New Ways to Lose
subheadline: Pete Crow-Armstrong had one of the great individual fantasy weeks of the season, and Canberra still went 0-1.
body:
## Thriller of the Week

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins def. Canberra Wombats, 5-4**

There is a version of this matchup that ends with the **Canberra Wombats** finally breaking through. They out-hit the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** in batting average, led in runs (32 to 27), led in home runs (10 to 8), led in RBI (34 to 22), tied in stolen bases (4 apiece), and posted a.388 OBP to the Twins'.285. By any reasonable offensive accounting, Canberra won this game. The scoreboard, as it has all season, disagreed.

The Twins did one thing well enough to survive: they threw a lot of innings and got outs efficiently enough to win the categories that mattered on the mound. Their 75.0 innings pitched was second-best in the league this week, their eight quality starts led the entire league, and while a 4.68 ERA is nothing to celebrate, it was enough to stack pitching points against a Canberra staff that pitched only 31 innings, posted a 6.10 ERA, and generated zero quality starts.

Jazz Chisholm chipped in 2 HR and 3 SB for the Twins, which was enough offensive contribution to avoid getting swept in hitting categories. But make no mistake: this was a victory earned entirely in the rotation. The **Wombats** dominated the lineup and still lost because their pitching staff cannot hold a lead, cannot eat innings, and cannot win categories that are right there for the taking. A 5-4 final feels almost dishonest given the actual production.

For Canberra, still winless on the season at 0-9-4, this was not a moral victory. It was another chapter in a genuinely painful story.

---

## The Week's Defining Moment

The number 82 tells you something important about what happened in Week 13. The **Hiroshima Radiation** pitching staff struck out 82 batters this week, the second-highest single-week strikeout total in league history, falling just ten short of their own record of 92 set in Week 12. That is not a hot streak. That is a rotation operating at a historically rarified level for the second consecutive week.

And yet none of that is the defining moment of Week 13. That distinction belongs, grimly, to Pete Crow-Armstrong and the **Canberra Wombats**.

Crow-Armstrong posted 4 HR, 7 RBI, 5 runs, and a.600 OBP this week. Read those numbers again. A.600 on-base percentage. Four home runs. Those are not just strong fantasy numbers; they are the kind of week that wins matchups outright in most formats. His performance headlined a Canberra offense that led the league in OBP at.388, out-ran, out-homered, and out-RBI'd the Twins by comfortable margins.

It did not matter. The Wombats' pitching staff threw 31 innings and posted a 6.10 ERA with zero quality starts. The categories the Wombats could not win on the mound were not close. The Twins took K, QS, IP, ERA, and WHIP almost by default, and those five pitching categories, plus a handful of stolen base and OBP splits, were enough to claim the match 5-4.

The season-long luck delta for Canberra is now the worst in the league. At some point, the label of "unlucky" stops fully covering it. The Wombats have a genuine offensive core. Until they address the rotation in any meaningful way, that core will keep performing into a void.

---

## Matchups

### The Upset: North Correa def. Athletic Park Dragons, 5-4

On paper, this matchup belonged to the **Athletic Park Dragons**. Their pitching staff posted a 2.93 ERA, the best in the league this week, along with five NSVH. Peter Lambert delivered a 1.29 ERA gem. Jake McCarthy contributed a.407 OBP. The Dragons held a clear structural advantage on the mound and were the better-constructed team coming into the week.

**North Correa** stole it anyway, and the reason was Jac Caglianone. The rookie-year breakout performance of 4 HR, 7 RBI, and 7 runs is the kind of singular offensive contribution that scrambles category math. North Correa's offense posted a.336 OBP and 8 HR to the Dragons' anemic.276 OBP and 6 HR, winning enough hitting categories to offset the pitching deficit.

The result stings for Athletic Park in a particular way because their ERA advantage (2.93 to 4.80) was real and substantial, but ERA does not win categories in a vacuum when the other team's hitters are clearing the bases. The Dragons remain structurally sound but their offense's inability to generate on-base percentage (.276 is a persistent problem) keeps costing them matchups they should control. The Week 14 showdown with the first-place **Albuquerque Bombers** will be an unforgiving test of whether that can change.

### Notable: Hiroshima Radiation def. Riverside Roughnecks, 6-4

The **Riverside Roughnecks** put up a genuinely impressive offensive week: 11 HR, 40 RBI (the highest RBI total in the league), and a.314 OBP with Nick Kurtz (3 HR, 9 RBI) and Travis Bazzana (3 HR, 7 RBI,.588 OBP) both producing at an elite level. They still lost 6-4 because one quality start from the rotation is not a viable pitching strategy. The **Radiation** swept the mound categories, threw 76.1 innings, and had enough offensive production from Kyle Schwarber (5 HR, 10 RBI, 9 runs,.444 OBP) to win even when the Roughnecks out-RBI'd them by eleven. Riverside's luck delta of +3.35 is the most inflated in the league. Regression is patient.

### Notable: Buttville Skidmarks def. Sugar Land Skeeters, 6-4

The **Sugar Land Skeeters** are going to keep losing matchups until they figure out their lineup, and this week illustrated the problem cleanly. Chase Burns posted a 0.90 ERA and 14 K. The staff as a whole put up a 3.57 ERA and five quality starts. CJ Abrams hit three home runs. And the Skeeters still lost because 5 HR and 17 RBI as a team is not enough offensive production to win categories against almost anyone. **Buttville** got 3 HR and 8 RBI from Wyatt Langford and a.600 OBP from Bryan Reynolds, which was enough. A Week 14 date with the Twins is a genuine opportunity for the Skeeters to right the ship.

### Quick Takes

**BaseballPro77 def. Wisconsin Rapids Reapers, 7-3.** Logan Gilbert posted an 18 K, 1.35 ERA, 0.60 WHIP anchor performance in 13.1 innings, Jeremy Pena added 3 HR, and Bobby Witt Jr. contributed a.563 OBP. The **Reapers** got 12 HR total (the highest in the league) and a.526 OBP from Juan Soto, but only 34.2 innings from the rotation sealed the match. **BaseballPro77** swept pitching categories with ease.

**Benchwarmers def. Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs, 8-2.** Corbin Carroll's 8 RBI, Zach Neto's 2 HR and 5 RBI, and a 0.98 WHIP from the staff made this comfortable. The **Wonderdogs** scored 37 runs but got there with only 6 HR and relied on only 29.2 innings from their pitchers, which is not a sustainable road to category wins.

**Albuquerque Bombers def. General Hospital, 8-1.** This was not close and was not meant to be. The **Bombers** got 9 HR, 34 RBI, and contributions from every corner of the roster (Herrera's.483 OBP, Buxton's 2 HR, Wheeler's 16 K and 1.54 ERA) against a **General Hospital** staff that posted a 5.02 ERA and 51 K. The gap between these two teams right now is considerable.

---

## Standings Watch

After thirteen weeks, the separation at the top of the standings is becoming harder to close. The **Albuquerque Bombers** sit at 9-3-1 with 76 points scored, and this week's 8-1 dismantling of General Hospital was less a victory than a demonstration. No team in the league combines pitching depth with offensive balance the way the Bombers do. Their Week 14 matchup with the **Athletic Park Dragons** (8-4-1, 66 PF) is the most important game on the schedule for the playoff picture, because a Dragons win would bring them back into legitimate first-place conversation, while a Bombers win would extend a separation that is already meaningful.

The **Riverside Roughnecks** at 9-3-1 have identical records to the Bombers but trail by three points-for, and their luck delta story is the biggest unresolved question in the league. They keep generating elite offensive weeks and keep losing pitching categories. How long can a team sustain a top-three record when its rotation posts one quality start per week? Week 14 against **North Correa** (5-6-2) will be revealing.

**BaseballPro77** at 9-4-0 has quietly positioned themselves as the third genuine contender after this week's win. Their Week 14 matchup with **Hiroshima Radiation** (6-5-2) is the single most analytically interesting game on the schedule. The Radiation have the most historically dominant strikeout rotation in the league right now. BaseballPro77 has Logan Gilbert. Someone will blink.

The middle of the standings is crowded in a way that makes every result matter. The **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** (7-6-0) sit in fifth despite a fortunate week. The **Sugar Land Skeeters** (6-6-1) have the pitching profile of a playoff team with the offensive output of a bubble team. **Hiroshima** at 6-5-2 feels underseeded given their rotation's performance.

At the bottom, the **Canberra Wombats** at 0-9-4 are running out of weeks. Mathematically, the season is not over. Practically, this is a team that needs to win nearly every remaining matchup to reach the postseason, and they have not won a single one yet.

---

## Lucky/Unlucky Team of the Week

**Lucky Team: Abbotsford-Colby Twins**

The **Twins** were out-run, out-homered, out-RBI'd, out-stolen-based (tied, actually, but the spirit holds), and out-OBP'd by the **Canberra Wombats** this week. They won 5-4. The victory was real, the standing it produced was real, and the points count. But a team that posts a.285 OBP as a unit and a 4.68 ERA should not be beating a team that posted a.388 OBP and led in five of the six offensive categories. The Twins survived on volume: 75 innings, eight quality starts, and enough pitching category wins to paper over an offense that is genuinely below average. The addition of Slade Cecconi and Grant Holmes signals that even the organization recognizes the rotation situation requires attention, even after a week that happened to reward it.

**Unlucky Team: Canberra Wombats**

There are not many ways to say this that have not been said across thirteen weeks of Wombats coverage, but here is the plainest version: **Canberra** led or tied in every offensive category this week, generated a.388 OBP (first in the league), 10 HR, 34 RBI, and 32 runs, and lost. Their cumulative luck delta is the worst single-week mark posted in the league this season. Pete Crow-Armstrong's week was one of the great individual performances in BeerLeagueBaseball history and it did not move the win column. Until the pitching situation is addressed with something more than marginal adjustments, this will keep happening, and the season will keep slipping away from a team that genuinely deserves better.

---

## Waiver Wire

No notable waiver wire activity was reported this week.

---

## Trade Value Watch

**Pete Crow-Armstrong (Canberra Wombats): SELL.** Crow-Armstrong posted 4 HR, 7 RBI, 5 runs, and a.600 OBP this week, which is one of the great individual fantasy outputs of the season, and none of it produced a win for his team. That is not a knock on him; it is precisely why his value is at its ceiling right now. A team sitting 0-9-4 that is not credibly in the playoff race is an ideal trade partner for a contender willing to overpay on a peak week. His production is real, his upside is real, and any competitor looking for an offensive boost heading into the final weeks of the regular season should be calling Canberra.

**Kyle Schwarber (Hiroshima Radiation): HOLD.** Five HR, 10 RBI, 9 runs, and a.444 OBP in a single week is an eye-popping line, but Schwarber has shown this kind of capacity before and the **Radiation** are not a team that needs to move him. He is the offensive centerpiece of a legitimate contender with the best pitching staff in the league right now. Selling into a peak week makes sense for rebuilding teams; for Hiroshima, holding through the playoff stretch is the obvious call.

**Travis Bazzana (Riverside Roughnecks): BUY.** Three HR, 7 RBI, and a.588 OBP in a week where his team lost suggests Bazzana is contributing at a level that is not fully reflected in the Roughnecks' standings position. If the luck delta corrects and Riverside's pitching normalizes even slightly, Bazzana is the kind of player who drives a very good offensive week. Buy before the market catches up with the underlying performance.

---

## Power Rankings

1. **Albuquerque Bombers** | An 8-1 demolition of General Hospital with balanced contributions across the entire roster (Herrera's.483 OBP, Buxton's 2 HR, Wheeler's 16 K and 1.54 ERA, and Freeman all delivering) confirms this is the most complete team in the league heading into a Week 14 clash with the Dragons. [SAME]

2. **Hiroshima Radiation** | Kyle Schwarber's absurd 5 HR, 10 RBI, 9 runs, and.444 OBP was one of the great individual fantasy weeks of the season, and 82 K from the pitching staff is historically elite; losing to the Roughnecks 6-4 is a minor blemish on a resume that still screams contender. [DOWN]

3. **BaseballPro77** | Logan Gilbert's 18 K, 1.35 ERA, and 0.60 WHIP in 13.1 innings was a dominant anchor performance, and Jeremy Pena's 3 HR plus Bobby Witt Jr.'s.563 OBP gave the offense real teeth in a 7-3 win over the Reapers; a Week 14 date with Hiroshima is the biggest matchup of the season. [UP]

4. **Riverside Roughnecks** | Nick Kurtz (3 HR, 9 RBI) and Travis Bazzana (3 HR, 7 RBI,.588 OBP) gave this team a legitimately elite offensive punch, but losing 6-4 to Hiroshima despite 40 RBI and 11 HR exposes continued pitching fragility with only 1 quality start; the luck delta of +3.35 remains the most inflated in the league. [DOWN]

5. **Athletic Park Dragons** | Peter Lambert's 1.29 ERA gem and Jake McCarthy's.407 OBP led a scrappy effort, but losing 5-4 to North Correa despite superior pitching metrics (2.93 ERA, 5 NSVH) stings; the offense's.276 OBP is a structural concern heading into a Week 14 showdown with the Bombers. [DOWN]

6. **Sugar Land Skeeters** | Chase Burns' 14 K and 0.90 ERA anchored a pitching staff that posted a 3.57 ERA and 5 QS, and CJ Abrams went deep 3 times, but a 6-4 loss to Buttville in which the offense managed only 5 HR and 17 RBI reveals the lineup's persistent inability to generate run support; a massive Week 14 matchup with the Twins looms. [SAME]

7. **North Correa** | Jac Caglianone's breakout (4 HR, 7 RBI, 7 runs) was one of the best individual performances of the week and carried North Correa to a 5-4 squeaker over the Dragons; zero stolen bases remains a recurring weakness, and Week 14 against the Roughnecks is a true measuring-stick moment. [UP]

8. **Buttville Skidmarks** | Wyatt Langford (3 HR, 8 RBI) and Bryan Reynolds (.600 OBP) were excellent, Kyle Bradish posted a 1.17 ERA, and 65 strikeouts led the league this week; a 6-4 win over the Skeeters is a solid result, and the rotation's 5.12 ERA is the only persistent red flag on an otherwise above-average roster. [UP]

9. **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Ben Rice and Kyle Stowers each slammed 3 HR with 6 RBI in a week that generated 12 HR total, and Juan Soto's.526 OBP was elite, but losing 7-3 to BaseballPro77 while posting only 34.2 IP and 2 QS reveals a team still dependent on opponents' pitching going cold. [SAME]

10. **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Jazz Chisholm's 2 HR and 3 SB provided needed pop and speed, but a.285 OBP as a team and a 4.68 ERA underscore why this was a fortunate 5-4 win; adding Slade Cecconi and Grant Holmes signals urgency around a rotation that needs immediate stabilization. [SAME]

11. **Benchwarmers** | Zach Neto (2 HR, 5 RBI), Kevin McGonigle (2 HR), and Corbin Carroll's 8 RBI were genuine offensive contributors in an 8-2 win over the Wonderdogs; a 0.98 WHIP from the pitching staff is legitimately impressive, and this feels like a team quietly steadying itself. [UP]

12. **General Hospital** | Dillon Dingler (2 HR,.423 OBP) and JJ Wetherholt (.483 OBP) had strong individual weeks, and Cam Schlittler's 0.67 WHIP was eye-catching, but an 8-1 blowout loss to the Bombers with only 51 K and a 5.02 ERA from the rotation tells the story of a team that cannot win pitching categories consistently. [SAME]

13. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Samad Taylor's 6 runs and 2 SB and Esteury Ruiz's.474 OBP gave the offense some life, and Dustin May's 11 K in 11 IP was efficient, but an 8-2 loss to the Benchwarmers with only 6 HR and 26 RBI reveals an offense that still cannot generate enough power; 37 runs scored but only 29.2 IP from the rotation is a dangerous imbalance. [SAME]

14. **Canberra Wombats** | Pete Crow-Armstrong's 4 HR, 7 RBI, 5 runs, and.600 OBP was one of the great individual weeks of the entire season and deserves every superlative, but a 6.10 ERA, 0 quality starts, and only 31 innings from the pitching staff handed a winnable matchup to the Twins; until this rotation is fixed, elite hitting will keep going to waste. [SAME]

---

## Looking Ahead

Three matchups on the Week 14 schedule are worth circling.

**BaseballPro77 vs. Hiroshima Radiation** is the headliner, a top-three collision between the two best pitching staffs in the league. Logan Gilbert against a Radiation rotation that has posted historically elite strikeout totals in back-to-back weeks is a genuine analytical puzzle, and Kyle Schwarber's bat will test whatever BP77 throws at him. This is the kind of matchup that reshuffles the playoff seeding picture regardless of which way it goes.

**Albuquerque Bombers vs. Athletic Park Dragons** is the more consequential game from a standings perspective. The Dragons need to prove their offense can generate OBP against a real opponent, and the Bombers would love nothing more than to confirm their first-place standing with a signature win over a contender.

**North Correa vs. Riverside Roughnecks** is the measuring-stick moment the power rankings teased. North Correa is riding momentum off a quality win. The Roughnecks have the offensive firepower to expose any rotation with vulnerabilities. If the Roughnecks' pitching collapses again, their position in the standings will start to feel genuinely precarious.

Week 14 has the shape of a genuine inflection point. Check back next week.
